title: Christmas Day
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: December 25, 2017

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on Christmas Day (The Nativity of Our Lord Jesus Christ), one of the two principal feasts of the Christian year. The RCL readings for Christmas Day (Year A) would have included Isaiah 52:7-10, Psalm 98, Hebrews 1:1-4 (5-12), and John 1:1-14, focusing on the Incarnation and the Word made flesh. The liturgical season was Christmas, following the four Sundays of Advent, and this sermon marked the culmination of the Christmas season's beginning.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the year following the 79th General Convention (held June 2015 in Salt Lake City), with no major denominational convention scheduled. The Diocese of Colorado (which includes Saint John's Cathedral as its mother church) was in the episcopate of Bishop Robert J. Wright, who had been serving since 2013. There were no major bishop elections or significant ecclesiastical controversies of note at this specific moment in the Episcopal Church.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver had experienced clear, cold weather in the days leading up to Christmas 2017, with temperatures in the 30s-40s Fahrenheit and no significant snow events the week prior. Locally, the focus was on the holiday season and year-end activities. The Cathedral, located in downtown Denver's Capitol Hill neighborhood, would have been preparing for its traditional Christmas services and community gatherings.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

President Trump's tax cuts (formally the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act) had just been signed into law on December 22, 2017, dominating political coverage throughout the week. The #MeToo movement continued gaining momentum, with major sexual harassment allegations against prominent figures in entertainment and politics emerging regularly. The NFL playoff race was in full swing with four weeks remaining in the regular season, with the Denver Broncos struggling at 5-10 record, unlikely to make playoffs.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Palestinian territories were experiencing heightened unrest following Trump's December 6 announcement recognizing Jerusalem as Israel's capital, with ongoing clashes in Gaza. North Korea had conducted its final ballistic missile test in November 2017, and diplomatic tensions remained high despite some communication openings. The Rohingya refugee crisis in Myanmar continued, with over 600,000 having fled to Bangladesh since August 2017.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The cultural zeitgeist was characterized by deep political polarization and social anxiety, with the country divided over Trump's presidency and agenda. Christmas 2017 offered a moment of traditional religious observance and family gathering against a backdrop of national turbulence and media saturation. The Broncos' disappointing season and general holiday season provided some respite from the contentious political and social news cycle that had dominated much of 2017.
